title: JavaScript Roadmap
description: Step by step guide to learn JavaScript in 2025

topics.name: All About Variables
topics.description: Most of the time, a JavaScript application needs to work with information. To store and represent this information in the JavaScript codebase, we use variables. A variable is a container for a value.

links.title: Storing the information you need — Variables

topics.name: Apply
topics.description: The apply() method of Function instances calls this function with a given this value, and arguments provided as an array (or an array-like object).

topics.name: Arguments Object
topics.description: The arguments object is an Array-like object accessible inside functions that contains the values of the arguments passed to that function, available within all non-arrow functions. You can refer to a function's arguments inside that function by usin

topics.name: Arithmetic Operators
topics.description: The Arithmetic operators perform addition, subtraction, multiplication, division, exponentiation, and remainder operations.

topics.name: Arrays
topics.description: Arrays are objects that store a collection of items and can be assigned to a variable. They have their methods that can perform operations on the array.

topics.name: Arrow Functions
topics.description: Arrow Function is a new way of creating functions with the '=>' operator with a shorter syntax.

topics.name: Assignment Operators
topics.description: An assignment operator assigns a value to its left operand based on the value of its right operand. The simple assignment operator is equal (`=`), which assigns the value of its right operand to its left operand. That is, `x = f()` is an assignment e

topics.name: Asyncawait
topics.description: `async/await` is a special syntax to work with promises in a more comfortable fashion. We use `async` keyword to declare a async function that return a Promise, and the `await` keyword makes a function wait for a Promise.

topics.name: Asynchronous Javascript
topics.description: Asynchronous programming is a technique that enables your program to start a potentially long-running task and still be able to be responsive to other events while that task runs, rather than having to wait until that task has finished. Once that tas

links.title: What The Hack is Event Loop and Asynchronous JavaScript - JSConf

topics.name: Bigint Operators
topics.description: Most operators that can be used with the `Number` data type will also work with `BigInt` values (e.g. arithmetic, comparison, etc.). However, the unsigned right shift `>>>` operator is an exception and is not supported. Similarly, some operators may

topics.name: Bigint
topics.description: BigInt is a built-in JavaScript object that allows you to work with integers of arbitrary size.

links.title: The Whys and Hows Of BigInt

topics.name: Bind
topics.description: The `bind()` method in JavaScript allows you to create a new function with a specific context and optionally preset arguments. Unlike `call()` or `apply()`, `bind()` does not immediately invoke the function. Instead, it returns a new function that ca

topics.name: Bitwise Operators
topics.description: Bitwise operators treat arguments as 32-bits (zeros & ones) and work on the level of their binary representation. Ex. Decimal number `9` has a binary representation of `1001`. Bitwise operators perform their operations on such binary representations,

topics.name: Block
topics.description: This scope restricts the variable that is declared inside a specific block, from access by the outside of the block. The let & const keyword facilitates the variables to be block scoped. In order to access the variables of that specific block, we nee

topics.name: Boolean
topics.description: In JavaScript, a `boolean` is a simple data type that can hold one of two values: `true` or `false`. These values are used to represent logical states and are essential in controlling the flow of a program.

topics.name: Break / Continue
topics.description: `break` statement, without a label reference, can only be used to jump out of a loop or a switch block.

topics.name: Built In Functions
topics.description: JavaScript offers a variety of built-in functions that simplify common tasks, available globally or within specific objects without requiring explicit definition. Functions like parseInt(), setTimeout(), and Math.random() can be used directly, while

topics.name: Built In Objects
topics.description: Built-in objects, or "global objects", are those built into the language specification itself. There are numerous built-in objects with the JavaScript language, all of which are accessible at the global scope. Some examples are:

topics.name: Call
topics.description: The `call()` method allows you to invoke a function with a given `this` value, and arguments provided individually.

topics.name: Callback Hell
topics.description: The callback hell is when we try to write asynchronous JavaScript in a way where execution happens visually from top to bottom, creating a code that has a pyramid shape with many **})** at the end.

links.title: Callbacks in Callbacks - Pyramid of Doom

topics.name: Callbacks
topics.description: A callback function is a function passed into another function as an argument, which is then invoked inside the outer function to complete some kind of routine or action.

topics.name: Classes
topics.description: Classes are a template for creating objects. They encapsulate data with code to work on that data. Classes in JS are built on prototypes but have some syntax and semantics that are not shared with ES5 class-like semantics.

topics.name: Closures
topics.description: Function closures are one of the most powerful, yet most misunderstood, concepts of JavaScript that are actually really simple to understand. A closure refers to a function along with its lexical environment. It is essentially what allows us to retur

topics.name: Comma Operators
topics.description: The comma operator (`,`) evaluates each of its operands (from left to right) and returns the value of the last operand. This lets you create a compound expression in which multiple expressions are evaluated, with the compound expression's final value

topics.name: Commonjs
topics.description: CommonJS modules are the original way to package JavaScript code for Node.js. Node.js also supports the ESModules standard used by browsers and other JavaScript run-times, but CJS is still widely used in backend Node.js applications. Sometimes these

links.title: How the CJS Module System Works
How to Import and Export Modules in CJS

topics.name: Comparison Operators
topics.description: Comparison operators are the operators that compare values and return true or false. The operators include: `>`, `<`, `>=`, `<=`, `==`, `===`, `!=` and `!==`

topics.name: Conditional Operators
topics.description: Conditional operator also known as Ternary operator is the only JS operator that takes three operands.

topics.name: Conditional Statements
topics.description: When you write code, you often want to perform different actions for different decisions. You can use conditional statements in your code to do this. In JavaScript, we have three conditional statements: `if`, `if...else`, and `switch`.

links.title: Making decisions in your code — conditionals

topics.name: Const
topics.description: Constants are block-scoped, much like variables declared using the `let` keyword. The value of a constant can't be changed through reassignment (i.e. by using the assignment operator), and it can't be re-declared (i.e. through a variable declaration)

topics.name: Control Flow
topics.description: In JavaScript, the `Control flow` is a way of how your computer runs code from top to bottom. It starts from the first line and ends at the last line unless it hits any statement that changes the control flow of the program such as loops, conditional

topics.name: Data Structures
topics.description: A Data structure is a format to organize, manage and store data in a way that allows efficient access and modification. JavaScript has primitive (built-in) and non-primitive (not built-in) data structures. Primitive data structures come by default wi

Introduction to the Queue Data Structure

topics.name: Data Types
topics.description: Data type refers to the type of data that a JavaScript variable can hold. There are seven primitive data types in JavaScript (Number, BigInt, String, Boolean, Null, Undefined and Symbol). Objects are non-primitives.

links.title: JavaScript data types and data structures

topics.name: Debugging Issues

topics.name: Debugging Performance
topics.description: Enter the dev tools and check out the Lighthouse tab. This is essentially a series of tests which analyses the currently open website on a bunch of metrics related to performance, page speed, accessibility, etc. Feel free to run the tests by clicking

topics.name: Default Params
topics.description: Default function parameters allow named parameters to be initialized with default values if no value or `undefined` is passed.

topics.name: Dom Apis
topics.description: With HTML DOM, JavaScript can access and change all the elements of an HTML document such as its attributes, CSS styles, remove elements, add and create new elements on the page. Web API means application programming interface for the web. All browse

topics.name: Dowhile
topics.description: The `do...while` statement creates a loop that executes a specified statement until the test condition evaluates to `false`. The condition is evaluated after executing the statement, resulting in the specified statement executing at least once.

topics.name: Equality Comparisons
topics.description: Comparison operators are used in logical statements to determine equality or difference between variables or values. Comparison operators can be used in conditional statements to compare values and take action depending on the result.

topics.name: Error Objects
topics.description: When a runtime error occurs, a new `Error` object is created and thrown. With this `Error` object, we can determine the type of the Error and handle it according to its type.

topics.name: Esm
topics.description: ESModules is a standard that was introduced with ES6 (2015). The idea was to standardize how JS modules work and implement these features in browsers. This standard is widely used with frontend frameworks such as react and can also be used in the bac

Full ESM module overview from js.info

topics.name: Event Loop
topics.description: The Event Loop is one of the most important aspects to understand about Node.js. Why is this so important? Because it explains how Node.js can be asynchronous and have non-blocking I/O, it explains the "killer feature" of Node.js, which made it this

topics.name: Exceptional Handling
topics.description: In JavaScript, all exceptions are simply objects. While the majority of exceptions are implementations of the global Error class, any old object can be thrown. With this in mind, there are two ways to throw an exception: directly via an Error object,

topics.name: Explicit Binding
topics.description: Explicit binding is when you use the `call` or `apply` methods to explicitly set the value of `this` in a function. Explicit Binding can be applied using `call()`, `apply()`, and `bind()`.

links.title: Explicit Binding rule for this keyword

topics.name: Explicit Type Casting
topics.description: Type casting means transferring data from one data type to another by explicitly specifying the type to convert the given data to. Explicit type casting is normally done to make data compatible with other variables. Examples of typecasting methods ar

topics.name: Expressions / Operators
topics.description: At a high level, an expression is a valid unit of code that resolves to a value. There are two types of expressions: those that have side effects (such as assigning values) and those that purely evaluate. The expression `x = 7` is an example of the f

topics.name: Fetch
topics.description: The `fetch()` method in JavaScript is used to request to the server and load the information on the webpages. The request can be of any APIs that return the data of the format JSON or XML. This method returns a promise.

links.title: Abort a fetch request manually in JavaScript

topics.name: For
topics.description: The `for` loop is a standard control-flow construct in many programming languages, including JavaScript. It's commonly used to iterate over given sequences or iterate a known number of times and execute a piece of code for each iteration.

topics.name: Forin Loop
topics.description: The for...in statement iterates over all enumerable properties of an object that are keyed by strings (ignoring ones keyed by Symbols), including inherited enumerable properties.

topics.name: Forof Loop
topics.description: The for...of statement executes a loop that operates on a sequence of values sourced from an iterable object. Iterable objects include instances of built-ins such as Array, String, TypedArray, Map, Set, NodeList (and other DOM collections), and the a

topics.name: Function Borrowing
topics.description: Function borrowing allows us to use the methods of one object on a different object without having to make a copy of that method and maintain it in two separate places. It is accomplished through the use of `.call()`, `.apply()`, or `.bind()`, all of

links.title: When would I use function borrowing

topics.name: Function Parameters
topics.description: The parameter is the name given to the variable declared inside the definition of a function. There are two special kinds of syntax: default and rest parameters.

topics.name: Function
topics.description: When a variable is declared inside a function, it is only accessible within that function and cannot be used outside that function.

links.title: Function Scope & Block Scope in JS

topics.name: Functions
topics.description: Functions exist so we can reuse code. They are blocks of code that execute whenever they are invoked. Each function is typically written to perform a particular task, like an addition function used to find the sum of two or more numbers. When numbers

topics.name: Garbage Collection
topics.description: Memory management in JavaScript is performed automatically and invisibly to us. We create primitives, objects, functions… All that takes memory. The main concept of memory management in JavaScript is reachability.

topics.name: Global
topics.description: Variables declared Globally (outside any function) have Global Scope. Global variables can be accessed from anywhere in a JavaScript program. Variables declared with `var`, `let` and `const` are quite similar when declared outside a block.

topics.name: History Of Javascript
topics.description: JavaScript was initially created by Brendan Eich of NetScape and was first announced in a press release by Netscape in 1995. It has a bizarre history of naming; initially, it was named Mocha by the creator, which was later renamed LiveScript. In 1996

topics.name: Hoisting
topics.description: JavaScript Hoisting refers to the process whereby the interpreter appears to move the declaration of functions, variables, or classes to the top of their scope, prior to execution of the code.

topics.name: How To Run Javascript
topics.description: JavaScript can be run in the browser by including the external script file using the `script` tag, writing it within the HTML page using the `script` tag again, running it in the browser console or you can also use [REPL](https://www.digitalocean.com

topics.name: Ifelse
topics.description: The `if` statement executes a statement if a specified condition is `truthy`. If the condition is `falsy`, another statement in the optional `else` clause will be executed.

topics.name: Iifes
topics.description: Immediately-Invoked Function Expression is a function that is executed immediately after it is created.

topics.name: Implicit Type Casting
topics.description: Implicit type conversion happens when the compiler or runtime automatically converts data types. JavaScript is loosely typed language and most of the time operators automatically convert a value to the right type.

links.title: What you need to know about JavaScript Implicit Coercion

topics.name: In A Function
topics.description: The keyword `this` when used in a function refers to the global object.

topics.name: In A Method
topics.description: Methods are properties of an object which are functions. The value of this inside a method is equal to the calling object. In simple words, this value is the object “before dot”, the one used to call the method.

topics.name: In Arrow Functions
topics.description: The keyword `this` when used in an arrow function refers to the parent object.

topics.name: In Event Handlers
topics.description: The keyword `this` when used in an event handler refers to the element that received the event.

topics.name: Indexed Collections
topics.description: Indexed Collections are collections that have numeric indices i.e. the collections of data that are ordered by an index value. In JavaScript, an array is an indexed collection. An array is an ordered set of values that has a numeric index.

topics.name: Introduction To Javascript
topics.description: JavaScript, often abbreviated JS, is a programming language that is one of the core technologies of the World Wide Web, alongside HTML and CSS. It lets us add interactivity to pages e.g. you might have seen sliders, alerts, click interactions, popups

topics.name: Islooselyequal
topics.description: [isLooselyEqual](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/JavaScript/Reference/Operators/Equality) checks whether its two operands are equal, returning a `Boolean` result. It attempts to convert and compare operands that are of different types.

topics.name: Isstrictlyequal
topics.description: [isStrictlyEqual](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/JavaScript/Reference/Operators/Strict_equality) checks whether its two operands are equal, returning a `Boolean` result. It always considers operands of different types to be different.

topics.description: Keyed collections are data collections that are ordered by key not index. They are associative in nature. Map and set objects are keyed collections and are iterable in the order of insertion.

topics.description: Modules encapsulate all sorts of code like functions and variables and expose all this to other files. Generally, we use it to break our code into separate files to make it more maintainable. They were introduced into JavaScript with ECMAScript 6.

topics.name: Null
topics.description: The `null` value in JavaScript signifies the deliberate absence of any object value. It is considered as one of JavaScript's primitive values and a `falsy` value.

topics.description: The Object.is() static method determines whether two values are the same value.

topics.description: The rest parameter syntax allows a function to accept an indefinite number of arguments as an array, providing a way to represent [variadic functions](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Variadic_function) in JavaScript.

topics.name: Set
topics.description: The `Set` object lets you store unique values of any type, whether [primitive](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Glossary/Primitive) values or object references. A value in the `Set` may only occur once; it is unique in the `Set`'s collection.

topics.name: Setinterval
topics.description: The `setInterval()` method helps us to repeatedly execute a function after a fixed delay. It returns a unique interval ID which can later be used by the `clearInterval()` method, which stops further repeated execution of the function.

topics.name: Settimeout
topics.description: The setTimeout runs a function after the specified period expires. Times are declared in milliseconds.

topics.description: The keyword `this` when used alone refers to the global object.
